name: Capitoline Hill
description: The Capitoline Hill, the smallest of Rome's seven hills, held immense significance in Roman mythology and state religion. It was the citadel (arx) of the earliest Romans and served as the religious center of the city. The hill housed the Temple of Jupiter Optimus Maximus, dedicated to Jupiter, Juno, and Minerva, which was the most important temple in ancient Rome and a symbol of Roman power. Triumphant generals would ascend the Capitoline Hill to offer sacrifices to Jupiter after their victories. The hill was also associated with Romulus, the founder of Rome, and its prominence in Roman history underscored its importance in the city's mythology and identity. Furthermore, the Capitoline Hill was not only a religious center but also a place of political importance, with many important decrees and pronouncements being made from its summit. The hill's enduring presence throughout Roman history cemented its place as a sacred and powerful symbol, forever linked to the city's founding and its rise to prominence. The legends and rituals associated with the Capitoline Hill shaped Roman identity and reinforced the connection between the city and its divine protectors. Archaeological evidence continues to reveal more about the hill's rich history and its vital role in Roman culture.
subtitle: Rome's Sacred Citadel and Religious Center

keyMyths.title: Foundation of the Temple of Jupiter
keyMyths.description: The construction of the Temple of Jupiter Optimus Maximus was said to have been initiated by Tarquinius Priscus and completed by Tarquinius Superbus. The temple's dedication was a major event in Roman history, symbolizing the city's power and connection to the gods.

keyMyths.title: Discovery of the Head (Caput)
keyMyths.description: During the construction of the Temple of Jupiter, a human head (caput) was supposedly discovered, which was interpreted as an omen of Rome's future dominion over the world. This event contributed to the hill's name and its association with power and destiny.

sources.author: Livy
sources.description: A comprehensive history of Rome from its founding.
